title: Choking and Foreign-Body Airway Obstruction Response
subtitle: The nurse responds to choking — letting a person with a mild block keep coughing, giving abdominal thrusts to an adult or child with a severe block, using back blows and chest thrusts for an infant, and starting CPR if they collapse.

patient.name: Choking Response
patient.admitDate: The nurse recognizes and responds to a foreign-body airway obstruction.

labs.v: No air; weak or no cough.

nursesNote: The nurse recognizes and responds to a foreign-body airway obstruction. The universal sign of choking is clutching the throat; the key is distinguishing a mild from a severe obstruction. With a mild (partial) obstruction the person can still cough, speak, or breathe, so the nurse encourages forceful coughing, stays with them, and does not interfere. With a severe obstruction the person cannot speak, cough, or breathe and may turn blue, so the nurse acts at once: for a responsive adult or child, abdominal thrusts (Heimlich) are given just above the navel with quick inward and upward motions; chest thrusts are used instead for a pregnant or very obese person. For a choking infant under one year, the nurse alternates 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts and never uses abdominal thrusts because of organ-injury risk. Thrusts continue until the object is expelled or the person becomes unconscious. If the victim collapses, the nurse lowers them, calls for help, begins CPR, and looks in the mouth before breaths, removing an object only if it is visible (no blind finger sweep). After relief, the person is assessed and may need evaluation for injury from the thrusts.
providerOrders: Encourage forceful coughing for a mild (partial) airway obstruction
Give abdominal thrusts to a responsive adult or child with a severe obstruction
Use chest thrusts for a pregnant or very obese person
Use 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts for a choking infant; no abdominal thrusts
Call for help and begin CPR if the victim becomes unconscious

opts.text: Clutching the throat is the universal sign of choking
A person who can speak and cough has a mild obstruction
Someone who cannot speak, cough, or breathe has a severe block
For a severe obstruction in an adult, give abdominal thrusts
For a choking infant, use abdominal thrusts
If the person can cough forcefully, do thrusts right away
Encouraging coughing is best when the airway is only partly blocked
Abdominal thrusts force air up to expel the object
Infants get back blows and chest thrusts, not abdominal thrusts
A blind finger sweep can push the object deeper
If a choking victim becomes unconscious, begin CPR
Keep doing thrusts even after breathing returns

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order responding to a conscious adult with a severe obstruction

opts.text: Confirm severe choking and ask 'Are you choking?'
Call for help
Stand behind and give abdominal thrusts above the navel
Repeat thrusts until the object is out or they collapse
If they go unconscious, lower them and start CPR
Use abdominal thrusts (Heimlich)
Give 5 back blows then 5 chest thrusts
Ask 'Are you choking?' before acting
Avoid abdominal thrusts to protect the organs
Stand or kneel behind and thrust above the navel
Support the head with the infant face-down on your forearm
Use chest thrusts if the adult is pregnant
Alternate back blows and chest thrusts until relieved

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: The choking adult collapses and becomes unconscious
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Lower the person safely to the floor
Call for help / activate the emergency response
Begin chest compressions (CPR)
Look in the mouth and remove an object only if visible
Continue CPR, attempting breaths, until help arrives
Encouraging a person who can cough to keep coughing
Doing abdominal thrusts on someone coughing forcefully
Giving abdominal thrusts to an adult who cannot breathe
Using abdominal thrusts on a choking infant
Starting CPR when a choking victim becomes unconscious
Doing a blind finger sweep to find the object
Using chest thrusts for a choking pregnant woman
Continuing thrusts after breathing returns

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Reduction of Risk Potential, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
